startScreenOptions.title: PY1.1-PY1.7 — Interactive Lecture (PY)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 5 embedded questions for PY1.1-PY1.7.

interactions.label: Which component of the cell membrane acts as a fluidity thermostat — reducing ex

params.question: Which component of the cell membrane acts as a fluidity thermostat — reducing excess movement at body temperature while preventing solidification at lower temperatures?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Phospholipids form the structural bilayer itself, but they do not regulate its fluidity. Their spontaneous arrangement creates the barrier; cholesterol modulates how fluid that barrier is.
Correct. Cholesterol inserts between phospholipid tails. At 37 °C it restricts excess movement, and at lower temperatures it prevents the bilayer from becoming rigid.
Integral proteins span the bilayer and serve as channels, carriers, receptors, and enzymes. They do not regulate membrane fluidity.
The glycocalyx is the carbohydrate coat on the outer surface. It mediates cell recognition and adhesion but has no role in regulating bilayer fluidity.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the Fluid Mosaic Model. Cholesterol sits between phospholipid tails and has a dual effect: it stiffens the membrane when it is too fluid and loosens it when it is too rigid.
Cholesterol is the membrane's fluidity buffer — it keeps the bilayer in a functional intermediate state across a range of temperatures, which is essential for proper channel and receptor function.

interactions.label: Sodium is the dominant cation in intracellular fluid, while potassium dominates

params.question: Sodium is the dominant cation in intracellular fluid, while potassium dominates extracellular fluid.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: The statement has the ions reversed. Potassium is the dominant intracellular cation, and sodium is the dominant extracellular cation. Remember the memory anchor from the video: K⁺ stays inside, Na⁺ stays outside. The Na⁺/K⁺ pump actively maintains this gradient.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — the statement is false. Potassium (K⁺) is the dominant intracellular cation (~140 mEq/L inside cells), while sodium (Na⁺) dominates the extracellular compartment (~140 mEq/L outside). This asymmetry, maintained by the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase, underpins the resting membrane potential and all electrical signalling.

metadata.title: Sodium is the dominant cation in intrace

interactions.label: The Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase moves three Na⁺ out and two K⁺ in per cycle. What is the prima

params.question: The Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase moves three Na⁺ out and two K⁺ in per cycle. What is the primary physiological consequence of this unequal ion exchange?

answers.text: It makes the cell interior more positive relative to the exterior

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The pump moves more positive charges out (3 Na⁺) than it brings in (2 K⁺). This creates a net loss of positive charge from inside the cell, making the interior more negative, not more positive.

answers.text: It generates a net negative charge inside the cell, contributing to the resting membrane potential

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Each cycle removes one more positive charge than it imports, making the pump electrogenic. This contributes directly to the resting membrane potential of approximately −70 mV.

answers.text: It equalises sodium and potassium concentrations across the membrane

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The pump does the opposite — it actively maintains the concentration difference by pushing Na⁺ out and pulling K⁺ in, sustaining the gradients that drive secondary active transport and electrical signalling.

answers.text: It reduces ATP consumption by recycling energy from the potassium gradient

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The pump consumes ATP — roughly 30% of the body's total ATP at rest. It does not recycle energy; it spends energy to maintain the ionic gradients that other transport processes then exploit.

overallFeedback.feedback: Remember: 3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in — that is one extra positive charge leaving the cell per cycle. This makes the pump electrogenic, contributing a small but important negative component to the resting membrane potential.

The electrogenic nature of the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase is fundamental. The net outward movement of positive charge contributes about −4 mV to the resting membrane potential and maintains the gradients that power secondary active transport (e.g., SGLT1 in ORS therapy).

interactions.label: A patient in diabetic ketoacidosis develops deep, rapid breathing (Kussmaul brea

params.question: A patient in diabetic ketoacidosis develops deep, rapid breathing (Kussmaul breathing). Which compensatory mechanism does this represent?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The bicarbonate buffer acts within seconds to mop up excess H⁺, but Kussmaul breathing is a respiratory response. The lungs blow off CO₂ to shift the bicarbonate equation and raise blood pH.
The kidneys are the most powerful pH regulators, but they take hours to days to respond. Kussmaul breathing begins within minutes of rising acidity — far too fast for renal compensation.
Correct. The respiratory centre detects falling pH and drives hyperventilation. Exhaling more CO₂ shifts the equilibrium: fewer H⁺ ions remain, and blood pH rises. This compensates within minutes.

answers.text: Protein buffering by haemoglobin inside red blood cells

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Haemoglobin is an important intravascular buffer, but it acts chemically within seconds. Kussmaul breathing is a respiratory motor response, not a chemical buffering reaction.

overallFeedback.feedback: Kussmaul breathing is the body's respiratory compensation for metabolic acidosis. By breathing deeply and rapidly, the lungs blow off CO₂, reducing the acid load within minutes. The kidneys are slower but more powerful, taking hours to days to fully correct the pH.

Kussmaul breathing is a classic example of respiratory compensation: the lungs expel CO₂ to counteract metabolic acidosis. Remember the three-tier speed hierarchy — chemical buffers (seconds), lungs (minutes), kidneys (hours to days).

metadata.title: A patient in diabetic ketoacidosis devel

interactions.label: When blood calcium falls below normal, which hormone is released first to restor

params.question: When blood calcium falls below normal, which hormone is released first to restore calcium levels?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Calcitonin does the opposite — it is released when calcium is too high. It inhibits osteoclasts and promotes renal calcium excretion, lowering blood calcium levels.

Correct. PTH is the first responder to hypocalcaemia. It stimulates osteoclast-mediated bone resorption, increases renal calcium reabsorption, and activates vitamin D production in the kidney to boost intestinal calcium absorption.

Calcitriol does increase intestinal calcium absorption, but it is not released first. PTH must first stimulate the kidney to convert 25-hydroxyvitamin D into active calcitriol. PTH is the primary signal; calcitriol is a downstream effector.
Insulin regulates blood glucose, not calcium. The calcium-regulating hormones are PTH (raises calcium), calcitonin (lowers calcium), and calcitriol (increases intestinal absorption).

overallFeedback.feedback: When calcium drops, the parathyroid glands release PTH. PTH then raises calcium through three actions: stimulating bone resorption by osteoclasts, retaining calcium in the kidneys, and triggering active vitamin D production, which increases gut absorption.

PTH is the body's primary defence against hypocalcaemia. It acts on three targets — bone (resorption), kidney (calcium retention + vitamin D activation), and indirectly on the gut (via calcitriol). This is a textbook example of negative feedback homeostasis.
